text: Q: What is Deal Hunter and what can it do? A: Deal Hunter is a voice shopping copilot. You say what you want to buy out loud, it searches the live web for current listings, ranks them cheapest first, and reads the best options back to you. It can also watch an item for you: tell it a target price and it will remember the item and target, and tell you where the price stands whenever you ask.

Q: Where do the prices come from, and are they live? A: Prices come from a live web search run at the moment you ask, not from a cached database or the model's training data. Deal Hunter searches current retailer listings through Bright Data's SERP API, extracts the price from each result, and ranks them cheapest first. Because listings change constantly, a price can still move between the search and your purchase.

Q: How does Deal Hunter know a price is real? A: After answering, it opens the top listing pages in the background through Bright Data's Web Unlocker and checks whether the price it quoted actually appears on the page. Confirmed prices are marked verified on screen. If a price cannot be found on the page it is marked unconfirmed, which means you should double-check before buying. Deal Hunter never rewrites a price by guessing from the page.

Q: How does watching an item work? A: Say something like watch this and tell me if it drops below one hundred thirty. Deal Hunter saves the product and your target price to its memory, so it persists across the conversation and future sessions. When you ask what you are watching, it re-checks the current price of each watched item and shows how far each one is from your target, flagging any that have hit it.

Q: Will Deal Hunter alert me on its own when a price drops? A: Not today. It does not poll prices in the background or send notifications. It re-checks prices when you ask what you are watching, and reports where each item stands. Treat the watchlist as something you check in on, not something that pages you.

Q: What does Deal Hunter remember about me? A: It remembers durable things you tell it, such as your name, preferred brands, sizes, general budget, and the items you asked it to watch. Memory is stored in a Moss index and scoped to your user id, so one person's watchlist and preferences are never visible to another. It does not store payment details.

Q: Can Deal Hunter buy something for me? A: No. It finds and compares listings and gives you the link to the cheapest one, but it never completes a purchase, enters payment details, or signs in to a retailer on your behalf. You make the actual purchase yourself.

Q: How should I phrase a request to get the best results? A: Name the specific product and model if you can, for example the cheapest Sony WH-1000XM5 rather than just headphones. You can add constraints like under two hundred dollars or with noise cancelling. If the request is vague, Deal Hunter will ask one short clarifying question before it searches.

Q: What happens if no prices are found? A: Some searches return listings with no clear price, for example review articles or out-of-stock pages. Deal Hunter will say it could not find a clear price rather than inventing one, and will offer to search again with different wording or open a specific listing to read the details.

Q: What technology does Deal Hunter run on? A: Three pieces, one job each. LiveKit runs the real-time voice: speech to text, the language model, and text to speech. Bright Data supplies the live web data, using its SERP API to find current listings and its Web Unlocker to read listing pages. Moss provides semantic retrieval and per-user memory, holding the watchlist and preferences. There is no database; the app is stateless apart from Moss memory.

Q: How accurate are the prices, and what are the caveats? A: Prices are parsed from live search results, so they reflect what retailers were advertising moments ago. They can still be wrong in a few ways: a snippet may show a promotional or starting-at price, a listing may be for a different variant or a refurbished unit, and shipping or tax is not included. That is why Deal Hunter opens the top listings to confirm, and marks anything it could not confirm.

Q: Is my data private? A: Your watchlist and remembered preferences are stored against your own user id and every recall is filtered to that id, so other users cannot see them. Deal Hunter does not ask for or store payment information, addresses, or account credentials.

Q: Can it compare two products? A: You can ask about one product at a time and then ask about another, and Deal Hunter will remember what you were looking at. For a direct comparison, ask for each product's best price in turn, then ask which is cheaper. It compares only prices it actually retrieved, never estimates.
